🎥 Bérénice Le Mestre 🎙️ @dario_borgognopic.twitter.com/M9CBrvgB7K

— M6 Info (@m6info) January 12, 2026

Across these cases, investigators see the same hallmarks: extreme violence, precise targeting based on digital wealth, and the use of cryptocurrency to obscure or launder stolen funds. The crimes exploit weaknesses in both financial oversight and personal data protection, creating a dangerous overlap between online exposure and physical vulnerability.

European authorities are stepping up cross-border cooperation to dismantle these networks, but the task remains daunting. Crypto transactions cannot be reversed, and tracing perpetrators across jurisdictions often takes time victims do not have.

Keep Your Crypto Assets Private

The Manosque kidnapping has forced a harsh reassessment of what it means to “secure” cryptocurrency. Experts stress that protecting digital assets now extends beyond passwords and firewalls into everyday personal behavior. Hardware wallets such as Ledger or Trezor remain critical, as do multi-factor authentication and encrypted, offline backups of private keys stored in separate physical locations.

If Ledger Wallet isn't the app at the center of your digital life, it should be.

• Buy, swap, stake, and earn using trusted providers.
• Rack up in-app rewards just for using core features.
• Link the CL Card to spend crypto anywhere and score 1% cashback in BTC or USDT.

A… pic.twitter.com/9AH8uC9FFj

— Ledger (@Ledger) December 28, 2025

Equally important is discretion. Security professionals increasingly warn investors against publicly disclosing crypto holdings, addresses or lifestyle signals that could attract attention. Some exchanges have begun offering insurance coverage and real-time alerts for suspicious transactions, while France’s National Cybersecurity Agency (ANSSI) and other European bodies are pushing cybersecurity education to help users reduce exposure.

The Manosque attack is a stark reminder that as cryptocurrencies move closer to the mainstream, they also attract increasingly violent forms of crime. What was once framed as purely a technological or financial risk is now, in some cases, a matter of physical survival.

The incident raises uncomfortable questions for policymakers and the industry alike. Should governments impose stronger safeguards around data privacy and crypto ownership, or does radical financial autonomy come with unavoidable personal risk?

For now, one thing is clear: in the age of digital assets, the line between online security and real-world danger is rapidly disappearing.

data.title: Crypto YouTube Views Plunge to Lowest Since 2021: Are Retail Traders Losing Interest?
data.description:
Crypto YouTube Engagement Hits Lowest Point Since Early 2021 Raising Questions About Retail Interest

Viewership of cryptocurrency content on YouTube has plunged to levels not seen since January 2021, reflecting a broader decline in retail engagement across social platforms.

After three months of steep falls, the momentum that had been slowly building in crypto-related online attention has largely reversed.

Is This a Widespread Social Media Retreat

Benjamin Cowen, founder of ITC Crypto, highlighted the trend on Sunday, sharing a 30-day moving average of views across multiple crypto YouTube channels.

He emphasised that the drop is not confined to YouTube, noting similar declines on X and other social platforms.

Cowen pointed to a wider disengagement in crypto communities, stating,

“So it’s not just X and an algorithm change.”

Here is a 30 day moving average of views to a lot of different crypto youtube channels.

So it's not just X and an algorithm change.

Viewership to crypto has been dropping across platforms. pic.twitter.com/zkowe3nb3L

— Benjamin Cowen (@intocryptoverse) January 11, 2026

Crypto commentator Tom Crown echoed the concern, stating that interest has “collapsed across all platforms, and has had a noticeable local decline since just October.”

He added,

“In reality, it’s been in a ‘bear market’ since 2021, having never reached even near those highs.”

It’s collapsed across all platforms, and has had a noticeable local decline since just Oct.
In reality it’s been in a ‘bear market’ since 2021 having never reached even near those highs pic.twitter.com/4EqzjeUS0S

— Tom Crown (@TomCrown) January 11, 2026

Bitcoin investor Polaris XBT described the current viewership as “literally bear market levels of social interest.”

Analysts note that this aligns with the notion that institutional investors are increasingly driving market movements, while retail traders remain largely inactive.

Retail Fatigue and the Toll of Scams

Content creators report similar patterns.

YouTube creator Jesus Martinez said,

“I’ve experienced some intense peaks, but nothing ever came close to the few videos I created in the peak of 2021.”

Indeed.

I grew my channel from the start of '22 & on

I've experienced some intense peaks but nothing ever came close to the few videos I created in the peak of '21

— Jesus Martinez (@JesusMartinez) January 11, 2026

TikTok creator Cloud9 Markets suggested that repeated scams and pump-and-dump schemes for speculative altcoins have exhausted retail audiences.

He also flagged a growing trend of wariness among individual traders, noting,

“Retail is tired of getting rekt.”

Stop the scam pump n dumps, every “altcoin” is pretty much a ponzi, without X/yt/tiktok shills there is zero reasons for any of the tokens to exist

Retail is tired of getting rekt -

Invest in quality assets, buy index funds if u too busy, buy gold

Enough of this crypto Bs

— Cloud9 Markets (@cloud9markets) January 12, 2026

Marc Shawn Brown, head of social media at Cointelegraph, pointed out that many investors are shifting focus toward traditional assets.

“They’ve likely pivoted into precious metals/macro. People want returns, not stories of when returns could come.”

He noted that in 2025, Bitcoin returned -7%, while palladium, rhodium, cobalt, silver, and gold outperformed the cryptocurrency, reducing incentives for speculative engagement.

Is Sentiment Slowly Stabilising Despite Lower Engagement

Bitcoin is shedding casual viewers but gaining grit.

According to Santiment, the social mood is finally bottoming out and turning bullish, as long as BTC can defend its $90,000 stronghold to keep retail faith alive.

In contrast, Ethereum is currently treading water.

The mood remains fractured and rudderless, perfectly capturing the "wait-and-see" paralysis that continues to stall the broader altcoin market.

Social Media Slump Signals Deep Shifts in Crypto Participation

Coinlive sees the sustained drop in engagement as a sign of deeper structural changes in the market.

Retail investors appear increasingly cautious, weary of repeated losses and speculative hype.

Institutions seem to be driving price action while social enthusiasm lags, raising questions about the durability of crypto’s retail-driven cycles.

The data suggests that future growth in crypto communities may depend less on sensational narratives and more on credible performance, transparency, and long-term market fundamentals.

This could redefine how content, sentiment, and investment behaviour interact in the next cycle.
